originalTitle: 'This tree was planted by my ancestor hundreds of years ago and my family settled here'
title: Africa's Super Old Tree: A Family's Amazing Story!
content:
Hello, amazing friends and future explorers across Africa! It's your kid-friendly news reporter, Auntie Ama, here! I have a super special story to share with you today. It's about a tree, a family, and a very, very long time ago in a beautiful country called Ghana!

Meet the Super Old Tree!

Imagine a tree that is older than your grandpa, and his grandpa, and his grandpa! This tree is so, so old. It lives in a lovely place in Ghana, near the big, blue ocean. It's a truly giant, strong tree. When you look at it, you can almost feel the whispers of time coming from its leaves. Its trunk is thick, so thick you'd need many friends to hug it all the way around! Its branches reach up to the sky, like giant arms saying hello to the sun. This amazing tree has seen so many sunrises and sunsets. It has felt the gentle rain and the warm African sun for hundreds and hundreds of years. It's not just a tree; it's like a living museum, full of secrets and stories.

A Family's Special Beginning

In a small town where people catch fish, there lives a very special family. They have a secret connection to this super old tree. They say their family started right here, in this very spot, because of this tree! This family has lived here for more generations than you can count on your fingers and toes! The tree has always been there, watching over them as they grow up, play, and live their lives. It’s like the tree is part of their family too. It’s always been there, giving shade and a special feeling of home.

What is 'Oral History'?

Now, how do they know their family started because of this tree? They know it because of something called 'Oral History'. It’s a bit like a special way of telling stories. 'Oral History' means that grandpas and grandmas tell their children, and those children tell their own children, and so on. It’s like passing down a treasured toy from one generation to the next, but with stories instead! These stories are very important. They help people remember where they came from and all the cool things their family did a long time ago. It's like having a big storybook in your head that never ends. Did you know? Many African cultures use oral history to share important lessons, songs, and traditions from the past to the future!

Way Back in Time: The 13th Century!

This special tree was planted a super, super long time ago. The stories say it was planted in the 13th Century. Can you imagine how long ago that was? It was before cars, before aeroplanes, and even before your great-great-great-grandparents were born! That was when kings and queens ruled in different ways, and life was very different from today. Think about it: when this tree was just a tiny seed, there were no phones or TVs! People used to travel by walking or riding donkeys. It was a time of exciting adventures and new beginnings.

Who Planted the First Seed?

The family’s oral history says that a brave man, their ancestor, planted this little seed. An ancestor is a family member who lived a very long, long time ago, before anyone you know was born. This ancestor was looking for a new place to call home. He travelled a long way, perhaps looking for good land or safe places to live. When he found this spot near the ocean, he felt it was just right. He planted a small seed, thinking of his future family. He hoped it would grow big and strong, just like he hoped his family would. And it did! Both the tree and his family grew wonderfully. Did you know? Ancestors are like the first pages in your family's storybook. They help us understand where we come from!

Life by the Sea: A Fishing Town

The town where this tree stands is a fishing town. This means many people who live there work with the ocean. They go out in their colourful boats to catch fish every day. Imagine the sun rising over the big ocean, and the sounds of the waves splashing. The fishermen bring their catches back to shore, and everyone helps prepare the fish for dinner or to sell in the market. For many years, this tree has watched the fishing boats come and go. It has seen generations of fishermen, all part of the family who settled because of its first seed. The tree offers a peaceful shade for people to rest after a long day at sea.

Why This Tree Matters So Much

This tree is more than just wood and leaves. It is a symbol of home. It showed the ancestor where to stop and build a life. It gave cool shade when the sun was hot, and its roots held the soil firmly in place. For the family, the tree is a reminder of their history. It reminds them of their ancestor's bravery and the beginning of their amazing family journey. It helps them feel connected to the past. It's a place where children still play, where elders tell stories under its wide branches, and where everyone feels a special bond. It's a landmark, a meeting spot, and a guardian of their town. Did you know? Trees are super important for our planet! They give us fresh air to breathe and are homes for many animals. They also help fight climate change!

Keeping the Story Alive

The family living there today still cares for the tree with great love. They make sure it stays healthy and strong. They know its story, and they tell it to their children, just as their ancestors did. This way, the special story of the tree and the family will never be forgotten. Each new generation learns about their roots and the amazing journey that started with just one small seed and one brave ancestor. It’s a beautiful way to honour their past and make sure their history lives on. The tree stands tall, a proud monument to their enduring legacy.

What Can We Learn?

This amazing story from Ghana teaches us a lot of important things. It teaches us that even a tiny seed can grow into something huge and important, just like a small family can grow into a big community. It teaches us the value of family stories and how important it is to listen to our grandpas and grandmas. Their memories are like treasures that help us understand who we are. And it reminds us to care for our planet, for the wonderful trees and all of nature. Because just like this special tree, they can be part of our stories for hundreds and hundreds of years to come. So let’s cherish our history and our beautiful African land!
summary: In Ghana, a very old tree planted in the 13th Century by an ancestor tells the amazing oral history of a family's beginnings in a fishing town.
whyMatters: This story matters because it shows us how old trees can connect families to their deep history, teach us about our ancestors, and remind us to cherish the amazing stories passed down through generations in Africa.

title: US imposes sanctions on DR Congo ex-President Kabila alleging rebel support
content: The former leader has not responded to US accusations that he is aiming to destabilise the government.

originalTitle: China scraps tariffs for all but one African nation
title: Good News for Africa! China Helps Our Countries!
content:
Welcome, Super Kids!

Hello, hello, hello! It's your friendly news reporter, Afia the Explorer, here! I'm so excited to share some really big and super cool news from our amazing continent, Africa! Get ready to hear about how a big friend from far away is helping us.

What's This Big News About?

Imagine you have a beautiful drawing, and you want to sell it. But then someone says, "You have to pay extra money just to sell your drawing!" That extra money is like a "tariff." It makes things more expensive. Well, guess what? A very big country called China has decided to say "NO MORE!" to this extra money for almost all countries in Africa! This means it will be easier and cheaper for our countries to sell their wonderful things to China. Isn't that amazing?

What Are Tariffs, Really?

Let's make it super simple. Imagine you have a yummy mango tree. You pick your mangoes and want to sell them to a shop in another town. Sometimes, the town might say, "To sell your mangoes here, you need to pay a little fee." This fee makes your mangoes cost more. That fee is a tariff. When a country, like China, buys things from another country, like Nigeria or Kenya, sometimes they have to pay a tariff. This tariff makes the things from Africa more expensive for China to buy. **Did you know?** Tariffs have been around for a very, very long time! Even ancient kings used to collect tariffs when traders came to their towns!

China Says "No More Extra Money!"

So, the big news is that China has decided to "scrap" these tariffs. "Scrap" means to take them away completely! It's like saying, "Hey Africa, you don't need to pay that extra fee anymore when you sell your amazing goods to us!" This is super good because now, things made in Africa – like yummy coffee, shiny minerals, beautiful clothes, or delicious fruits – will be cheaper for China to buy. And when things are cheaper, more people want to buy them! Think of it like this: If your toy is cheaper, more of your friends might want to buy it from you, right? Same for countries!

Who is China, Our Big Friend?

China is a very, very big country far away from Africa, across the ocean. It has lots and lots of people, even more than all the people in Africa put together! China makes many things, like toys, phones, and clothes that we use every day. China and Africa have been friends for a long time. They help each other. Africa sends natural resources like oil, metals, and agricultural products to China. And China sends many things we need back to Africa. **Did you know?** China is the most populated country in the whole world! That means it has more people living there than any other country!

Almost All African Countries Get This Help!

This amazing news is for almost all the countries in Africa! There are 54 countries in Africa, and almost all of them will get to sell their goods to China without paying these extra tariffs. Imagine a big party, and almost everyone gets a special treat! That's what's happening. This means many, many people across Africa will feel the good effects. Farmers, factory workers, and even governments will benefit. Only one country in Africa is not included in this "no extra money" deal right now. But don't worry, the important thing is that most of our continent is getting this big help!

Why Is This So Good for Africa?

This is fantastic news for our continent! Here's why: 1. **More Money for Our Countries:** When African countries can sell more things to China, they make more money. This money can be used to build new schools, new hospitals, better roads, and even help people start new businesses. 2. **More Jobs:** When more goods are sold, more people are needed to make them! This means more jobs for our moms, dads, brothers, and sisters. 3. **African Products Become Popular:** Our special things, like coffee from Ethiopia, cocoa from Ghana, or spices from Tanzania, will become even more popular in China because they are now cheaper and easier to buy. It's like getting a special boost for our economies – making them stronger and healthier!

What is "Soft Power"? Being a Kind Friend!

You might hear grown-ups talk about "soft power." What does that mean? Imagine you want your friends to like you. You could be kind, share your toys, and help them with their homework. When you do nice things, your friends like you more, right? They want to be friends with you. That's a bit like "soft power" for countries. When China removes tariffs and helps African countries, it's being a kind friend. This makes African countries like China even more, and want to work with China more. It's about being friendly and helpful, not about being strong or bossy. It shows that China cares about Africa and wants to see our continent grow and do well. **Did you know?** Many countries try to be good friends with other countries. It helps everyone!

What About "Uneven Gains"?

Some clever people who study countries and money (we call them "analysts") say that while this is great news, some African countries might benefit a bit more than others. Think of it like this: If you and your friend both get a new toy, maybe your friend's toy is slightly bigger or does something extra special. Both are good, but one might get a little more. This could happen because some African countries produce more of the things China wants to buy. So, they might sell more and make more money. But even if the gains are a little "uneven," it's still good news for everyone involved! Every little bit of help is a step forward. It means we should keep working hard to make sure all our countries grow strong.

A Bright Future for Africa!

So, there you have it, my super kid reporters! This news means a brighter future for Africa. Our countries can sell more, earn more, and grow stronger. It's a wonderful example of how countries can work together as friends to help each other. Let's celebrate this big news and keep learning about how our amazing continent connects with the whole world! I'm Afia the Explorer, signing off from your kid-friendly news desk! See you next time!
summary: China has stopped asking for extra money (tariffs) on things bought from almost all African countries, making it easier and cheaper for Africa to sell its goods and grow stronger.
whyMatters: This is important because it means African countries can sell more products, earn more money to build schools and hospitals, and create more jobs for people, helping our continent become even better!

originalTitle: 'If we sleep they bite': Rats and weasels infest camps for displaced Gazans
title: Hello from Africa! Nasty Pests in a Faraway Land!
content:
Jambo, amazing kids from all over Africa and the world! It's your friendly news reporter here, ready to share an important story with you. Today, we're going to talk about some very tiny creatures that are causing big problems for children and grown-ups in a place far away called the Gaza Strip.

Meet Your Reporter

I'm so happy to be with you today! I love to tell stories about what is happening around our big, beautiful world. We learn new things every day, and that's super cool!

Where is the Gaza Strip?

The Gaza Strip is a place that is very, very far from most of our homes here in Africa. It's a small piece of land near a big blue sea. Many people live there. Did you know? The world is a giant ball, and we live on one part of it, and the Gaza Strip is on another part!

People Who Lost Their Homes

Right now, many children and families in the Gaza Strip have had to leave their homes. They didn't want to leave, but they had to. So, they are living in special places called 'camps'. A camp is like a big village made of tents or small shelters. It's not like living in a nice, strong house with a proper kitchen and bathroom. Imagine if you had to leave your house and live in a tent. It would be very different, wouldn't it? It can be hard to keep everything clean and tidy.

Tiny Troubles: Rats!

In these camps, a new challenge has come up. It's all about some tiny animals that are causing trouble. The first one is a rat. What is a rat? A rat is a small animal, a bit like a mouse, but bigger. It usually has grey or brown fur and a long tail. Rats like to hide. Did you know? Rats are very good at squeezing into small spaces, even tiny holes! Rats love to find food. They can eat almost anything! They can chew through things to get to food, like bags or boxes.

Tiny Troubles: Weasels!

The other little trouble-maker is called a weasel. Have you ever heard of a weasel? A weasel is also a small animal. They are usually long and thin, with short legs. They have brown fur and are very, very fast! Weasels are a bit like a cat, but wilder and much smaller. They are very quick hunters. Did you know? Weasels can hunt for their food during the day and night! They are super speedy. Both rats and weasels are living very close to people in these camps. This is not good because they can cause problems.

Other Pests Too!

It's not just rats and weasels. Other tiny pests are making life difficult too. Pests are small creatures that are annoying or can cause harm. Think about flies and mosquitoes. Flies like to land on food and can spread germs. Mosquitoes can bite you and make you feel sick. Did you know? Mosquitoes are tiny, but they can carry very serious sicknesses. That's why we try to keep them away!

If We Sleep, They Bite!

The people in the camps say, "If we sleep, they bite." This means that these animals are around all the time, even at night when everyone is trying to rest. Imagine trying to sleep, but you worry about a rat or weasel coming close. It would be very scary and hard to get good sleep. These animals can bite people. A bite from a rat or a weasel can hurt. It can also make you sick.

Why Are These Pests a Problem?

Pests like rats and weasels carry tiny, tiny things called 'germs'. You cannot see germs with your eyes because they are so small. Did you know? Germs are like invisible tiny monsters that can make us cough, have a fever, or get a tummy ache! When these animals touch food, or bite someone, or leave their mess, they can spread these germs. Then, the germs can make people sick. And when many people live close together, sickness can spread very fast. This is a big problem in the camps because people already have a lot to worry about. They need to stay healthy.

Eating Food and Making a Mess

These pests also try to eat people's food. They get into bags and boxes where food is kept. This means there is less food for the children and families. They also leave their mess everywhere. This makes the camps even harder to keep clean. And a dirty place is where germs love to live and grow! Did you know? Keeping your hands clean by washing them with soap and water is one of the best ways to stop germs from making you sick!

What Can Help?

It's very hard to get rid of all the pests when you live in a camp. But people try their best. They try to keep food covered very well. They try to clean up quickly after eating. Sometimes, they can use special traps to catch the pests. But it's a big, big job when so many animals are around. This story helps us remember that keeping clean is important for everyone, everywhere. Even in our own homes in Africa, we must try to keep our places clean so pests don't cause problems.

A Message of Hope

It's a tough situation for the children and families in the Gaza Strip. We hope that one day soon, they will have safe, clean homes where they don't have to worry about rats and weasels at night. Remember, we can all help by being kind, learning about others, and keeping our own communities healthy and clean. Thank you for listening, amazing kids! Until next time, stay curious and stay kind!
summary: Children and families living in camps in the Gaza Strip are facing a new challenge as rats, weasels, and other pests are bothering them, spreading germs, and making it hard to stay healthy.
whyMatters: This is important because everyone deserves to live in a safe and clean place, and when tiny animals spread sickness, it makes life much harder and scarier for children and families who have already lost their homes.
